4 min read
Just now

--

‚ÄúJust use git add, then git commit."
That‚Äôs probably the first Git advice every developer hears.
But here‚Äôs the problem.
Most Git tutorials jump straight into commands without explaining why Git works the way it does.
Over the past 10+ years, I‚Äôve worked with Git almost every day ‚Äî designing workflows, helping teams collaborate, and mentoring engineers. One pattern keeps showing up: many developers can use Git well enough to get by, but when something unexpected happens, confidence disappears.
A merge conflict appears.
A commit goes missing.
Someone accidentally resets a branch.
Suddenly, Git feels like black magic.
In my experience, that rarely happens because Git is difficult. It happens because we learn the commands before we learn the mental model.
So before we write our first git add or git commit, let's understand how Git actually thinks.
Git isn‚Äôt a backup tool
One misconception I see surprisingly often is that Git stores your files.
It doesn‚Äôt.
Git stores snapshots of your project.
Imagine taking a photo of your project every time you reach a meaningful milestone.
Instead of remembering every tiny file change, Git remembers the entire state of your project at that moment.
That‚Äôs why Git can move backward and forward through history so efficiently.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Mental model: Git is a snapshot machine, not a backup folder.
The six concepts that make Git make sense
Everything you‚Äôll learn in Git builds on these six concepts.
Think of them as the foundation. Once these click, the commands start feeling obvious.

1. Repository ‚Äî Git‚Äôs memory
A repository is where Git stores everything it knows about your project: commits, branches, tags, and the complete history.
A common mistake is thinking a repository is just a folder with a .git directory. Technically that's true, but mentally it's more useful to think of it as Git's memory. It remembers every snapshot you've chosen to save.

3. Staging Area ‚Äî Your packing list
This is probably Git‚Äôs most misunderstood feature.
People often ask:
‚ÄúWhy do I need git add? Why can't Git just commit everything?"
Because not every change belongs in the same commit.
Maybe you fixed a bug and reformatted a few unrelated files. You probably don‚Äôt want those bundled together.
The Staging Area lets you decide exactly what belongs in the next snapshot.
I like to think of it as a packing list before a trip. You choose what goes into your suitcase before you close it.
Once you start thinking this way, git add stops feeling like an extra step and starts feeling like a useful one.

Repository4. Commit History ‚Äî Your project‚Äôs timeline
Every commit is a snapshot with a message explaining what changed.
Together, those commits form a timeline.
Initial Commit
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Add Login Page
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Fix Authentication
‚îÇ
‚ñº
Improve UIThis timeline is what makes Git so powerful. You can inspect it, compare points in time, return to earlier versions, or understand how a feature evolved.
5. HEAD ‚Äî Your current position
HEAD sounds mysterious, but the idea is simple.
HEAD tells Git:
‚ÄúThis is where I am right now.‚Äù
Most of the time, HEAD points to the latest commit on your current branch.
That‚Äôs it.
We‚Äôll explore HEAD in much more detail later, but for now, just remember that it‚Äôs Git‚Äôs way of tracking your current location in the project‚Äôs history.
Putting it all together

Working TreeIf you understand this diagram, you‚Äôve already built the mental model that every Git command relies on.

One thing I‚Äôve learned over the years
When developers say:
‚ÄúGit is confusing.‚Äù
What they usually mean is:
‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know where my changes are.‚Äù
Are they still in the Working Tree?
Have they been staged?
Have they already been committed?
Once you can answer those three questions, you‚Äôll find that many Git problems become much easier to solve.

If this article helped you understand Git a little better, consider sharing it with a teammate who‚Äôs just getting started. There‚Äôs a good chance it‚Äôll save them from spending hours trying to figure out why Git isn‚Äôt doing what they expected.
